description.s: Investigate whether Automerge is the right storage foundation for Hobson's next phase: system-level undo + mobile sync.
Context
Two upcoming needs converge on the same problem:
System-level undo.
Deleting an item, removing an attachment, changing a type are all irreversible. The system needs a way to revert destructive operations.
Mobile sync.
Hobson needs to be accessible on mobile, which requires syncing data between devices with conflict resolution.
Automerge is a CRDT library that potentially solves both: its operation log gives history/undo, and its CRDT foundation enables sync with automatic conflict resolution.
Questions to Answer
Data model fit.

Hobson items are JSON documents with nested objects, arrays, and Active Text ASTs. Can Automerge represent these naturally? What changes to the mutation patterns (api.set, api.attach, etc.) are needed?
Storage overhead.

Automerge stores a full operation log. Hobson items are typically set as whole field values, not character-by-character. What's the realistic storage cost for Hobson's usage pattern? Is it acceptable at 1,000+ items?
Migration path.
Can the current storage API (get, set, delete, query) be preserved as a facade over Automerge? How would existing IndexedDB items be migrated?
Undo ergonomics.
How does undo work in practice? Can you revert a single item's last change without affecting other items? What about reverting a delete (item no longer exists as a document)?
Sync architecture.
What does the sync layer look like? automerge-repo? Custom sync server? Peer-to-peer? What's the minimum viable setup for two-device sync?
Alternatives.
Is Automerge the best option, or should Yjs, PouchDB+CouchDB, or a simpler custom approach be considered? What are the tradeoffs?
What We Know So Far
Automerge supports maps, lists, text (with rich-text marks), strings, booleans, null, integers, floats, counters, timestamps, bytes — a superset of JSON.
Mutations must go through Automerge's change API (can't assign plain JS objects directly).
Storage overhead: ~4-6 bytes per character for text editing, likely 2-3x raw JSON for Hobson's whole-field-value pattern.
History is preserved by default (compaction repackages but doesn't discard). Full operation log enables time travel.
PouchDB was also considered but its revision system is designed for sync conflict resolution, not version history. Old revision bodies are discarded on compaction.
